voiceover: I used to think being offline was temporary. It's not. An accountant came back from two weeks away — dozens of site visits, zero contact details. Visitors had real questions. The website just stood there, silent. No reply. No capture. Nothing. Your site is open 24/7. Your answers aren't. That's not a gap — that's a permanent leak. Every night. Every weekend. Every vacation. Create your AI assistant.
title: The Mascot That Waved at Traffic While Your Leads Walked Away
concept: A sweating mascot waves at passing cars in a parking lot — cheerful, useless, completely offline. Cut inside: a chat widget shows a blinking typing indicator with no message ever sent. Then: an analytics screen silhouette showing dozens of sessions, zero conversions, zero contacts. The mascot is still out there waving. Nobody answered. The video closes with a quiet, confident call to action — your website can answer even when you can't.
script: OPEN (0–2s): Wide shot — a mascot costume figure wilting in bright parking-lot heat, waving at traffic. Nobody stops. BEAT 1 (2–6s): VO: 'I used to think being offline was temporary. It's not.' Cut inside a bright co-working space — a chat widget on a monitor shows a typing indicator, blinking endlessly, no reply ever sent. BEAT 2 (6–13s): VO: 'An accountant came back from two weeks away — dozens of site visits, zero contact details. Visitors had real questions. The website just stood there, silent.' Single person at a desk, back to camera, staring at analytics silhouette — many sessions, zero follow-ups visible in body language. BEAT 3 (13–19s): VO: 'Your site is open 24/7. Your answers aren't. That's not a gap — that's a permanent leak. Every night. Every weekend. Every vacation.' Quick cut back to the mascot, still waving, sun higher, nobody stopping. CLOSE (19–23s): VO: 'Create your AI assistant.' Warm, still frame — empty co-working desk, a soft glow on a monitor suggesting something is now active and ready.

This is the part most small business owners miss: being offline isn't a temporary gap. It's a permanent, invisible leak in your pipeline that runs every night, every weekend, and every time you step away. A contact form is not a safety net. It's a polite way to let qualified visitors leave quietly.

Most small business owners think lead loss is about traffic. It isn't. It's about what happens after someone lands on your site at 11 PM and has a question nobody answers. They don't fill out a form. They don't wait. They move on. The accountant who came back from vacation to zero contact details didn't have a traffic problem — she had a silence problem. Her site was there. Her answers weren't. That distinction matters more than almost any marketing tactic.
